ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general 'chicken curry (dish)' class; no specific Kali Mirch variant exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific Kali Mirch curry node. A00GJ (cooked chicken meat) is the closest defensible anchor; spice/sauce matrix is not separately coded.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept found for Chicken Kali Mirch or Indian black pepper chicken curry.

main_ingredients: whole spices (bay leaf, cloves, cardamom, cinnamon)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Chicken Kali Mirch is approximately 300 g, comprising bone-in or boneless chicken pieces in a black-pepper-forward sauce, usually accompanied by naan or rice.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken curry, Indian style — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 revised edition), Table entry for chicken curry
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN reports a standard North Indian chicken curry (bone-in, cooked with oil, onion, tomato, and spices). Chicken Kali Mirch is a variant of this preparation distinguished primarily by a high black-pepper load and reduced tomato. Macronutrient profile is closely analogous; sodium is adjusted upward slightly to reflect restaurant-level seasoning. Values are per 100 g edible portion.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Chicken Kali Mirch. GI estimated at 30 (low) by analogy with cooked chicken curry dishes and high-protein, low-carbohydrate meat-based preparations documented in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021. The dish is predominantly protein and fat with minimal carbohydrate (~3.5 g/100 g), yielding a low glycemic load. GL per 300 g serving = (30 × 10.5 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 3.2.

hypertension.guidance: A 300 g restaurant serving of Chicken Kali Mirch contributes approximately 1260 mg sodium, representing over half the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation or limit portion size.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate content and low glycemic index
type2_diabetes.guidance: With approximately 3.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g and an estimated GI of 30, Chicken Kali Mirch has a low glycemic load and is unlikely to cause rapid postprandial glucose excursions, making it a suitable protein-rich option for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-GI accompaniments such as white rice or naan.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat and sodium from oil, ghee, and restaurant seasoning
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Chicken Kali Mirch contains approximately 2.9 g saturated fat and 420 mg sodium per 100 g; individuals with cardiovascular disease should choose preparations using minimal ghee or oil, request lower-sodium seasoning, and pair the dish with fibre-rich vegetables rather than refined-carbohydrate sides.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chicken Kali Mirch (Hindi: चिकन काली मिर्च; also known as Murgh Kali Mirch) is a North Indian chicken curry distinguished by its bold, pungent black-pepper (kali mirch) flavour profile. The dish consists of bone-in or boneless chicken pieces marinated in yogurt and spices, then sautéed with onion, ginger, garlic, whole spices (cardamom, cloves, bay leaf, cinnamon), and a generous quantity of freshly cracked or coarsely ground black pepper. Unlike tomato-heavy North Indian curries, Kali Mirch relies on a yogurt-and-onion base, producing a pale, creamy, pepper-forward sauce with moderate heat and aromatic depth. The texture is tender and juicy, with a slightly thick, clinging gravy. Nutritionally, the dish is high in protein (~15.8 g/100 g), moderate in fat (~10.2 g/100 g), and low in carbohydrate (~3.5 g/100 g), giving it a low glycemic index (estimated ~30) and low glycemic load per serving. Sodium content is elevated in restaurant preparations (~420 mg/100 g), which is clinically relevant for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is naturally gluten-free and is commonly served halal. It is typically accompanied by naan, roti, or steamed basmati rice. In Singapore, it is served across multiple North Indian restaurants and is priced in the SGD 17–19 range per portion.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Chicken Kali Mirch?
Is Chicken Kali Mirch good for diabetics?
Is Chicken Kali Mirch high in sodium?
Chicken Kali Mirch vs Butter Chicken

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from the ICMR-NIN Indian chicken curry entry, which is the closest approved primary source for this North Indian dish. Chicken Kali Mirch is a recognised variant of North Indian chicken curry with a distinct spice profile but a closely analogous macronutrient composition. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021, as no direct GI measurement for this specific dish exists in the approved literature. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN (2017) North Indian chicken curry data; no exact ICMR-NIN entry for Chicken Kali Mirch exists. 2. Sodium is adjusted upward from the ICMR-NIN baseline to reflect typical restaurant-level seasoning observed across Singapore field survey listings. 3. GI is an analogous estimate; a direct peer-reviewed GI measurement for Chicken Kali Mirch has not been located in the approved source set. 4. FoodOn and FoodEx2 codes are closest defensible matches; no exact ontology node for this dish exists. 5. Halal status is assumed based on the restaurant context (North Indian Muslim-style cuisine); individual restaurant certification should be verified. 6. Price range SGD 17.90–18.90 is preserved from the field survey data source record (8 restaurants, April 2026). 7. vitamin_d_mcg is reported as -1 because vitamin D is not measured or reported in the ICMR-NIN (2017) chicken curry source entry; this nutrient is not present in the approved source data for this food and the value is therefore absent rather than zero. This is a documented missing-data case, not a confirmed zero.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Built full RESULT JSON from input seed record. Added ICMR-NIN adapted nutrient values, GI analogous estimate from Atkinson et al. 2021, FoodOn/FoodEx2 ontology anchors with gap notes, multilingual names (Chinese simplified/traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi), comprehensive aliases with aliases_by_language consistency check, three-condition health_context with Type 2 Diabetes human_condition_name, and LLM description exceeding 200 characters.

Iteration 3: Addressed G3 REJECT from Iteration 2 validator. Expanded the notes field to include an explicit, detailed missing-data basis statement for vitamin_d_mcg = -1, clarifying that vitamin D is not measured or reported in the ICMR-NIN (2017) chicken curry source entry and that -1 represents a documented missing-data case rather than a confirmed zero. All other sections preserved unchanged as they passed validation.
